items.prompt_md: You get an **unsorted** array of integers and must return the **positions** of two entries that add up to a target. What do you reach for first?
options: Walk two pointers inward from both ends
Keep a hash map of values seen so far and look up each complement
Slide a variable-width window across the array
Sort the array, then binary-search for each complement
items.answer: Keep a hash map of values seen so far and look up each complement
items.explanation_md: **Unsorted + return original indices + O(n)** is the hash-map complement fingerprint. Two pointers need a sorted array, and sorting would scramble the positions you must report.

items.prompt_md: An array is **already sorted ascending**. Find two values that sum to a target, using only **O(1)** extra space. Which technique fits best?
options: Keep a hash map of values seen so far and look up each complement
Walk one pointer from each end, moving them based on the sum
Expand and shrink a window over the array
Recurse with memoization over index and remaining target
items.answer: Walk one pointer from each end, moving them based on the sum
items.explanation_md: **Sorted + O(1) space** is the tell for opposite-ends two pointers: too-small sum → advance the left pointer, too-large → retreat the right. A hash map would work but spends O(n) memory the sortedness lets you avoid.

items.prompt_md: Find the length of the **longest substring with no repeating characters** in a string. What shape does the solution take?
options: A fixed-size window swept once across the string
A variable-size window that grows, and shrinks from the left on a repeat
Sort the characters and scan for runs
A hash map from each character to its global frequency
items.answer: A variable-size window that grows, and shrinks from the left on a repeat
items.explanation_md: **"Longest substring such that a condition holds"** is a dynamic sliding window: extend the right edge, and when the window breaks the no-repeat invariant, pull the left edge in until it holds again.

items.prompt_md: Given heights of vertical lines, pick two lines that with the x-axis hold the **most water**. Best approach?
options: Two pointers at the ends, always moving the shorter line inward
A sliding window of fixed width
A hash map of height to index
Dynamic programming over subranges
items.answer: Two pointers at the ends, always moving the shorter line inward
items.explanation_md: Area is bounded by the **shorter** line, so moving the taller one can never help — advance the shorter pointer. It looks window-ish, but it's opposite-ends two pointers converging.

items.prompt_md: Group a list of words so that all **anagrams of each other** land in the same bucket. What organizes the buckets?
options: A hash map keyed by each word's canonical form (e.g. its sorted letters)
Two pointers comparing words end to end
A sliding window over each word
Binary search after sorting the whole list
items.answer: A hash map keyed by each word's canonical form (e.g. its sorted letters)
items.explanation_md: Anagrams share one invariant — the same multiset of letters. Reduce each word to a **canonical key** and bucket by it in a hash map. "Group by an equivalence" ⇒ hashing.

items.prompt_md: Find the maximum sum of any **exactly k consecutive** elements in an array of numbers. Which is the O(n) way?
options: A fixed-size window of width k, adding the entering and dropping the leaving element
A variable-size window that grows while the sum is small
A hash map of prefix sums
Two pointers from both ends
items.answer: A fixed-size window of width k, adding the entering and dropping the leaving element
items.explanation_md: A **fixed** length ‘k consecutive’ is the fixed-size sliding window: slide by one, add the new element, subtract the one that fell off — O(n), no recomputation.

items.prompt_md: Decide whether an array contains **any duplicate value at all** (just yes/no). Cheapest reliable approach?
options: Insert into a hash set and report a collision
Two pointers scanning inward
A sliding window of growing size
Sort, then two pointers
items.answer: Insert into a hash set and report a collision
items.explanation_md: "**Have I seen this before?**" over an unordered collection is a hash-set membership check — one pass, O(n). Sorting also works but costs an extra O(n log n) for a question set membership answers directly.

items.prompt_md: In an array of **positive** integers, find the length of the **shortest contiguous subarray whose sum is ≥ target**. What fits?
options: A variable-size window that grows to reach the target, then shrinks from the left
A hash map from value to index
Two pointers from opposite ends of the array
Sort the array first, then scan
items.answer: A variable-size window that grows to reach the target, then shrinks from the left
items.explanation_md: Positive values mean growing the window only ever **raises** the sum and shrinking only lowers it — a monotonic relationship a dynamic window exploits. Sorting would destroy the contiguity the problem is about.
